summary:
The presence of bilateral duct dilation—main pancreatic and common bile duct—with progressive jaundice suggests an obstructive cause originating proximally. Carcinoma of the head of the pancreas best explains these symptoms and radiological findings due to its direct effect on biliary outflow through compression.

Final Answer: $\boxed{\text{Carcinoma of the head of the pancreas}}$

Okay, I need to summarize only what Agent2 reasoned about clinically regarding the case provided.

Key elements include: progressive jaundice, pruritus, clay-colored stool; history of fluctuating jaundice; CT showing dilation of both main pancreatic and CBD – which indicates upstream obstruction. 

This combination strongly implies malignancy involving either the pancreatic head or peripancreatic area that causes mechanical blockage affecting both ducts simultaneously. Given anatomical considerations where tumors near the ampulla can affect both channels directly via pressure-induced stenosis, especially when there’s no evidence suggesting other etiologies like gallstones or strictures without prior cholelithiasis risk factors mentioned here...

Therefore, based solely upon typical presentation patterns linked specifically with tumor-related obstructions rather than inflammatory conditions or benign pathologies known not typically presenting with persistent dual-duct enlargement over time…

I conclude that carcinoma arising within or adjacent to the head region most plausibly accounts for all observed manifestations—the hallmark triad being jaundiced appearance combined with altered bowel coloration plus abnormal imaging results indicating central canal expansion caused by external compression forces acting against normal flow pathways during digestion processes.

So ultimately focusing strictly just on core diagnostic logic used:

Dilation of both major intrahepatic drainage routes signals significant upper gastrointestinal tract pathology resulting primarily from structural compromise occurring early along their course toward exit point(s). In older adults who smoke heavily—a well-known high-risk factor—and exhibit slow progression alongside intermittent episodes suggest underlying neoplastic disease instead of transient issues commonly seen elsewhere across spectrum disorders related to liver function decline etc., making malignant growth particularly probable as root origin despite absence of overt metastasis indicators currently available but still consistent enough medically accepted standards under current contextual evaluation framework applied widely throughout gastroenterology practice settings worldwide today.

Thus final determination rests firmly rooted around recognition that simultaneous pancrease-bile system congestion reflects localized lesion pressing down on passageways responsible for transporting digestive secretions efficiently between organs involved in nutrient absorption stages post-meals while also contributing significantly towards development of visible skin irritation experienced by patients suffering prolonged exposure durations beyond expected recovery periods after initial diagnosis phase completion.
